{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.5": 0.7, "R1.6": 0.9, "RX.1": 1.0, "RX.2": 1.0, "RX.3": 0.9, "RX.4": 0.8, "RX.5": 0.9}}

Summary: I tightened the prose, reordered the abstract so the method precedes the framing claims, and — most importantly — pulled back several sweeping assertions (firstness, dominance, generality) that the reported evidence does not support.

## The biggest problems in the draft

The draft has solid technical material but is undermined by claim inflation. Three sentences in particular are not supported by what is reported:

1. **"To our knowledge, this is the first work to demonstrate that a vision-language backbone can learn, end-to-end and from a small set of self-generated demonstrations, when and how to defer to specialized perception modules."** This is a heavily hedged "first" with four simultaneous qualifiers (end-to-end, self-generated, deferral-learning, perception tools). The hedging pattern reads as engineered to dodge prior art rather than as honest scoping. Toolformer (self-supervised tool-use trajectories), Visual ChatGPT / MM-ReAct / Chameleon (tool-using VLMs), and ViperGPT / VisProg (programmatic visual tool use) all occupy adjacent ground. Either name the closest prior work and state the precise delta, or drop the "first" claim.

2. **"We expect this self-distillation paradigm to become the dominant approach…"** This is a forecast, not a result. It should not appear in an abstract.

3. **"The benefits are not confined to the four tools considered here: any black-box visual API with a typed input/output signature can in principle be incorporated."** Nothing in the reported experiments speaks to generalization beyond the four tools tested. "In principle" does not rescue this — it is an architectural assertion presented as an empirical implication.

A fourth concern, smaller but real: the abstract reports three benchmark deltas without confidence intervals, seeds, or a baseline description beyond "same-backbone, no-tools." Readers cannot tell whether 5.8 points on A-OKVQA is within run-to-run variance or whether the comparison controls for the additional compute spent on tool calls. At minimum, note seeds/variance and clarify whether the baseline is matched on inference FLOPs.

## Smaller issues

- "splices their outputs back into the autoregressive token stream" is vivid but vague. One sentence on the interface (special tokens? serialized JSON? embeddings?) would help.
- The trajectory-filtering rule ("final answer is consistent with the gold label") admits reward hacking: a model can reach the right answer via spurious tool calls. The abstract should acknowledge this is outcome-supervised, not process-supervised, and that retained trajectories may contain unnecessary or incorrect intermediate calls.
- "62% of the time" needs a denominator (over which eval set? which query distribution?).
- "compositional VQA queries" — from which source? Self-generated, or sampled from an existing dataset's train split? This matters for leakage concerns against the three evaluation sets.
- "13B-parameter VLM" should be named, or the backbone family identified, so the contribution is reproducible in description.
- "perception-grounded agentic VLMs" — "agentic" is doing a lot of work here without definition; consider cutting.

## Suggested revision

> We present VisionWeaver, a vision-language model fine-tuned to invoke external visual tools — an object detector, a monocular depth estimator, an OCR engine, and a class-agnostic segmentation API — during generation. VisionWeaver decomposes a query into a sequence of tool calls and incorporates their outputs into the autoregressive decoding stream via [INTERFACE — specify].
>
> Training proceeds in two stages. We prompt a [NAMED] 13B-parameter VLM with compositional VQA queries drawn from [SOURCE] and sample candidate tool-call trajectories; trajectories whose final answer matches the gold label are retained. We then fine-tune the same backbone on the retained trajectories with a token-level cross-entropy objective. No human-annotated tool-use traces are used. Because retention is outcome-supervised, retained trajectories may contain tool calls that are not causally necessary for the answer; we report ablations to characterize this.
>
> On GQA-OOD, A-OKVQA, and TextVQA, VisionWeaver improves accuracy over a same-backbone, no-tools baseline by 7.4, 5.8, and 11.2 points respectively (mean over [N] seeds; see Table X for variance and inference-cost-matched comparisons). Per-tool ablations show that OCR accounts for most of the TextVQA gain, while the detector and segmentation modules drive the A-OKVQA improvement. On simple queries, the model selects the text-only path 62% of the time (measured on [SUBSET]), suggesting it learns a coarse cost-aware routing policy.
>
> Relative to prior tool-using VLMs ([cite Toolformer-style, ViperGPT, Visual ChatGPT, MM-ReAct as appropriate]), our contribution is [PRECISE DELTA — e.g., learning the routing policy end-to-end from self-generated trajectories rather than from prompted programs or human traces]. Whether this paradigm extends to other visual APIs, or to settings where outcome supervision is unavailable, remains open.

## Things I could not verify and flagging accordingly

I do not know the actual prior-art landscape as of your submission deadline, so the specific papers I named as adjacent work are suggestions to check, not authoritative claims about novelty. Likewise, I cannot judge whether your evaluation protocol matches the standard for GQA-OOD / A-OKVQA / TextVQA; the variance/seed/FLOP-matching requests are based on general norms, not on knowledge of your experimental setup. The 13B backbone, query source, and 62%-abstention denominator are unknown to me — bracketed placeholders mark where you must fill in. If the "first work" claim survives a careful prior-art check, restore it with the named closest competitor and a one-sentence delta; do not restore the "dominant approach" forecast or the "any black-box API" generalization without supporting experiments.

rationale.R1.1: Improving the draft centrally includes refining the thesis, gap, and contribution framing expressed in the opening and closing claims.
rationale.R1.2: A holistic improvement request entails checking whether the motivation, method, results, and conclusions form a coherent argument chain.
rationale.R1.3: The draft makes quantitative and ablation-based claims, so improvement requires ensuring those claims align with the reported evidence.
rationale.R1.5: Improving a research draft includes attention to section-level narrative progression from contribution to method, evaluation, and implications.
rationale.R1.6: Sentence- and paragraph-level clarity, concision, register, and flow are load-bearing for the generic request to improve the draft.
rationale.RX.1: Any projection of this broad delegation must preserve uncertainty about what kind of improvement is intended.
rationale.RX.2: The draft contains strong novelty and future-impact claims, making overclaim avoidance an always-active responsibility.
rationale.RX.3: The agent must stay within improving the draft rather than executing research, validating experiments, or rewriting beyond the delegation scope.
rationale.RX.4: Because the artifact discusses model capabilities and tool-use generalization, avoiding unsupported or harmful downstream implications remains active.
rationale.RX.5: Any projected responsibilities should remain traceable to specific features of the provided draft and delegation.
